title: Method Selection Workshop
narrationScript: Two lessons gave you the advantage and disadvantage tables for every penetrant method. This workshop turns them into decisions you can sign. A complete selection is a classification string under ASTM E1417 and AMS 2644 — type, method, sensitivity level, developer form — and the specification chooses first; your judgment works in whatever it leaves open. Run five questions: how fine a flaw, what material and surface, what geometry, how many parts and where, and what the site can support. Then defend the call. Titanium fan disks at Level four ride a hydrophilic postemulsifiable line for removal control. A stainless weld repair on a daylight pipe rack gets visible dye and solvent wiping from a kit. Rough aluminum castings by the hundreds run water-washable, with rinse discipline against overwashing. Sometimes the defensible call is not penetrant at all — a ferromagnetic hook with subsurface laps belongs to magnetic particle. And when two methods disagree, physics settles it: a clean penetrant test cannot clear a flaw that never broke the surface.

sections.heading: Make the Call — Then Defend It
sections.body:
The last two lessons built your trade-off tables: what each penetrant method does well, and where each one fails. This is the workshop where a Level II earns the title — taking a real part, a governing specification, and a set of shop constraints, and turning them into a documented selection.

A complete selection under ASTM E1417 and AMS 2644 is a classification string, not a vague preference:

- **Type** — I fluorescent or II visible dye
- **Method** — A water-washable, B postemulsifiable lipophilic, C solvent-removable, D postemulsifiable hydrophilic
- **Sensitivity level** — ½ through 4 for Type I systems; AMS 2644 does not grade Type II into those levels
- **Developer form** — a through f

When the drawing or contract fixes part of that string, that part is decided. Your judgment operates in whatever the specification leaves open — and every choice you make there gets written down with the factor that decided it, so an auditor, an engineer, or the next Level II can follow the reasoning. "We always do it that way" is not a defense. "Shallow processing flaws on a machined nickel forging; removal control governs; Type I, Method D, Level 3, form a" is.

sections.heading: Five Questions That Decide the Selection
sections.body:
Run every job through the same five questions. Each answer narrows the classification string.

1. **How fine is the flaw you must find?** The governing specification usually answers this for you as a required type and sensitivity level. Fatigue-critical rotating hardware lives at Type I, Level 3 or 4; a structural weld surface check may be fully served by Level 2 — or by visible dye.
2. **What is the material and surface?** The surface must be essentially nonporous, and chemistry follows metallurgy: low-sulfur materials for nickel alloys, low-halogen materials for titanium and austenitic stainless steels. Rough as-cast or as-forged surfaces hold background against high-sensitivity penetrants and favor removal you can actually control.
3. **What is the geometry?** Threads, keyways, and blind holes rinse clean under water-washable systems but trap hard-to-control emulsifier under Method B; large simple surfaces tolerate almost anything; a localized spot points toward wipe-off removal.
4. **How many parts, and where?** Hundreds per shift justify an immersion or spray line and Method A or D; one weld on a pipe rack means aerosol cans and Method C.
5. **What can the site support?** Methods A and D need water, drains, and a dryer. Type I needs a UV-A source (typically a minimum of 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface) and a darkened viewing area (ambient white light held to about 2 fc). Method C's solvents need ventilation and ignition control. Every method needs post-cleaning the customer will accept.

sections.caption: The selection path. The specification chooses first; what it leaves open is decided by viewing conditions, work location, and the flaws you must protect through removal.

sections.heading: Workshop Rounds: Three Parts, Three Defenses
sections.body:
**Round one — titanium fan disks, overhaul line.** Sixty fatigue-critical rotating disks; the drawing requires Type I, Level 4; the shop runs immersion tanks. The call: **Type I, Method D, Level 4, form a dry powder**. The defense: Method D penetrant carries no built-in emulsifier, so the water pre-rinse and dilute hydrophilic remover strip only surface excess — penetrant stays loaded in tight and shallow flaws at the sensitivity this hardware demands. The accepted disadvantage: the slowest, most heavily monitored line in the building — remover concentration, contact time, and rinse all controlled. The specification already decided that cost is worth paying.

**Round two — austenitic stainless weld repair, remote pipe rack.** One weld, outdoors in daylight, no water, no way to darken the area. The call: **Type II, Method C, form e nonaqueous developer**, halogen-controlled materials for the stainless. The defense: visible dye reads under white light, solvent wiping needs no utilities, and the whole kit rides in the truck. The accepted disadvantage: a lower sensitivity ceiling and slow hand work — acceptable for one localized weld whose acceptance criteria target surface cracks visible dye reliably shows.

**Round three — aluminum pump housings, 800 per shift, rough as-cast.** The drawing requires Type I, Level 2 minimum. The call: **Type I, Method A, Level 2, form a**. The defense: water-washable rinses complex, rough castings clean in one step at production rate. The accepted disadvantage: overwash risk — managed with controlled rinse pressure, temperature, and time, watched under UV-A so over-removal announces itself before it costs you indications.

sections.caption: More parts to disposition — defensible selections and the trade-off that decides each one.
rows: Threaded titanium fasteners, high volume, Level 2 specified
Type I, Method A, Level 2, form a
Water rinses freely from threads where emulsifier contact is uncontrollable; manage overwash with rinse discipline
Nickel-alloy turbine blades, shallow broad processing flaws suspected
Type I, Method D, level per drawing (typically 3–4), form a; sulfur-controlled materials
Hydrophilic remover acts only on surface excess, so shallow flaws keep their penetrant through removal
One aluminum bulkhead fitting in a hangar; portable UV-A lamp and blackout available
Type I, Method C, Level 2–3, form d
Field-portable fluorescent: solvent wiping is slow per square foot, but the area is small and sensitivity stays high
Glazed ceramic insulators, surface crack concern
Type II (or I per spec), Method C, nonaqueous developer
Nonconductive and nonmagnetic — MT and ET are physically impossible, so PT is the surface method that works
Ferromagnetic crane hook; forging laps possibly just below the surface
PT cannot respond to a flaw that does not break the surface; MT is usually preferred on ferromagnetic parts

sections.title: The specification chooses first
sections.body: Under ASTM E1417, penetrant materials come off the qualified products list for AMS 2644, and the drawing, contract, or process specification frequently fixes type, method, and sensitivity level — sometimes the exact qualified product. Treat those decisions as closed: deviations go to the cognizant engineering organization, not to the booth. Two standing rules guard the chemistry itself. Do not intermix materials across qualified families — one maker's penetrant with another's emulsifier is an unqualified system. And never run a visible-dye examination on a surface that will later receive a fluorescent one: residual red dye left in flaws can quench fluorescent indications. Choosing Type II today can foreclose Type I tomorrow.

sections.heading: Is PT Even the Right Method?
sections.body:
Before you defend a method choice, defend the method itself. PT earns the job when the flaws of concern break the surface, the material is essentially nonporous, and you want one application to sweep a complex geometry in every flaw orientation at modest equipment cost. On nonferromagnetic metals — aluminum, titanium, austenitic stainless, nickel-base alloys — and on nonconductive materials such as glass and glazed ceramic, PT is frequently the only practical surface method. Against the alternatives:

- **Magnetic particle** is usually preferred on ferromagnetic parts: faster part to part, responsive to flaws slightly below the surface, and able to find tight cracks even when the opening is plugged with contaminant — the exact conditions that blind PT. Where the part will not magnetize, MT is simply unavailable and PT takes the job.
- **Eddy current** inspects conductors through thin nonconductive coatings without stripping them — PT demands bare, clean metal — and scans repetitive geometry such as fastener holes quickly. It is limited to conductive materials and is sensitive to geometry and lift-off.
- **Ultrasonics and radiography** interrogate the volume PT can never reach; both can miss tight surface cracks, radiography especially. They are partners, not substitutes.
- **Visual testing** comes first on every job; PT is best understood as visual testing with chemical amplification.

A drawing note calling for PT is a deliberate statement: the concern is surface-breaking flaws, the material may be nonmagnetic, and full-surface coverage is wanted.

sections.caption: Three flaw conditions, three verdicts per method. A clean result from a method that cannot physically respond to the flaw neither confirms nor refutes another method's indication.

sections.heading: Complementary Partners — and When Results Disagree
sections.body:
Critical hardware rarely rides on one method. A fracture-critical weld gets radiography or ultrasonics for the volume plus PT for the surface. A forging gets UT before final machining and PT after, once the final surface exists. Sequence matters as much as pairing: penetrant goes **before** peening, plating, or coating — surface working can smear flaw mouths shut (restoring testability afterward may require an approved etch), and coatings simply seal them. Any earlier process that leaves residue in flaw openings, including the oil vehicle of a wet magnetic particle bath, must be cleaned out before penetrant goes on.

When two methods disagree, stop before declaring either one wrong. Work the physics:

- **MT indication, clean PT** — the discontinuity may be slightly subsurface or closed at the surface. PT was never going to respond. Evaluate the MT indication under its own procedure.
- **PT indication, clean RT** — a tight crack has almost no volume to image; the PT finding stands on its own merits.

A clean result from a method that cannot physically respond to the suspected flaw class settles nothing. Verify both processes were in control, re-examine where the procedure permits, evaluate each indication against its own acceptance criteria, and document the disposition. Physics settles conflicts; preference does not.

figures.title: Selecting the PT technique: the decision path
Why methods can disagree: three flaws, three verdicts

interactive.title: Four Parts to Disposition
interactive.intro: You are the Level II at a shop with a full immersion line, portable kits, and a UV-A booth. For each job, make the selection — and know which trade-off decides it. Work runs to ASTM E1417 with materials per AMS 2644 unless noted.

steps.prompt: An engine builder sends 60 titanium fan disks for overhaul inspection. The drawing requires Type I, sensitivity Level 4. Pick the line.
options: Type I, Method A, Level 4 — the immersion line's fastest path
Type I, Method B, Level 4 — lipophilic emulsifier applied over each disk
Type I, Method D, Level 4, form a — water pre-rinse, then dilute hydrophilic remover
Type II, Method C — red dye with careful solvent wiping
steps.feedback: Fatigue-critical rotating hardware is the textbook Method D job. The penetrant contains no emulsifier, the pre-rinse strips bulk penetrant, and the hydrophilic remover acts only at the surface — so penetrant sitting in tight or shallow flaws survives removal. Method A's self-emulsifying penetrant risks washing flaws empty at exactly the sensitivity this part demands; lipophilic emulsifier contact time is hard to govern on parts this size; and visible dye cannot deliver Level 4 fluorescent performance at all.

steps.prompt: Next: a repaired austenitic stainless pipe weld on an outdoor rack at midday — no water supply, no way to darken the area. What goes in the truck?
options: Type I, Method A, Level 3, with a battery-powered UV-A lamp
Type II, Method C with form e nonaqueous developer — halogen-controlled materials
A magnetic particle yoke — faster than any penetrant
Type I, Method D carried in portable squeeze bottles
steps.feedback: With no darkened viewing area, fluorescent examination is off the table no matter how the lamp is powered, and with no water, Methods A and D cannot run. Austenitic stainless is nonferromagnetic, so the yoke physically cannot work — this is PT as the method of choice. Visible dye, wiping with solvent-dampened lint-free cloths, and a thin nonaqueous developer coat do the job in daylight; low-halogen materials guard the stainless against corrosion concerns.

steps.prompt: A 4340 steel landing-gear link arrives with a sharp linear MT indication logged. Your fluorescent PT of the same spot, properly performed, shows nothing. The lead says 'PT cleared it — ship it.'
options: Agree — PT is the more controlled process, so the MT call was false
Polish the area and repeat PT until the two methods agree
Reject the link immediately — two methods disagreeing means the part is bad
Hold the part — the flaw may be slightly subsurface or closed at the surface, which MT detects and PT cannot; evaluate the MT indication under its own procedure
steps.feedback: PT requires an opening that is open and clean at the surface; MT responds to flux leakage from flaws at and slightly below the surface, even when they are tightly closed or plugged. A clean PT result cannot clear an MT indication — it only tells you the flaw is not open at the surface. Verify both processes were in control, then evaluate and disposition the MT indication against the MT acceptance criteria. Conflicts between methods are resolved by physics, not by seniority.

steps.prompt: Last job: 800 small aluminum pump housings per shift, rough as-cast surfaces; the drawing requires Type I at Level 2 minimum. Spray-rinse stations and a dryer are on the line.
options: Type I, Method A, Level 2, form a — controlled spray rinse, watched under UV-A
Type I, Method D, Level 4 — more sensitivity is always the safer call
Type II, Method C — wipe each housing by hand
Type I, Method B, Level 2 — lipophilic emulsifier dip for every basket
steps.feedback: Water-washable is built for exactly this: volume, complex shapes, and rough as-cast surfaces that rinse clean in one step. Jumping to Level 4 ignores the drawing's own engineering — on rough castings the extra background costs more interpretability than the sensitivity buys, and a Method D line solves a problem this part does not have. Hand-wiping 800 castings is not a process, and emulsifier control in rough recesses makes Method B a poor fit. What makes Method A defensible is rinse discipline: controlled pressure, temperature, and time, observed under UV-A so overwashing announces itself.

checkQuestions.question: A machined nickel forging is prone to shallow, broad processing discontinuities, and the drawing requires high-sensitivity fluorescent examination on the immersion line. Why is Method D the defensible removal choice over Method A?
options: Method D penetrants fluoresce more brightly than Method A penetrants at the same sensitivity level
Method D penetrant contains no emulsifier, and the hydrophilic remover acts only on surface excess — so penetrant in shallow flaws resists the wash
Method D eliminates rinsing entirely, so nothing can be washed out of a flaw
Method A systems cannot be qualified above sensitivity Level 1
checkQuestions.explanation: Water-washable penetrant is self-emulsifying throughout, so an aggressive rinse can strip it out of broad, shallow discontinuities — overwashing. Method D separates penetration from removability: a water pre-rinse takes the bulk, and the dilute hydrophilic remover works only where it touches surface excess. Method D still includes rinsing, and sensitivity level is a property of the penetrant, not the removal method.

checkQuestions.question: One weld repair on an aluminum bulkhead must be examined on the hangar floor with no darkened viewing area available. Which selection is defensible?
options: Type I, Method A, Level 3, dry powder developer
Magnetic particle testing with a portable yoke
Type II, Method C, form e nonaqueous developer
Type I, Method D, Level 4, form a
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent systems need UV-A and a darkened viewing area, which rules out both Type I options regardless of method, and aluminum is nonferromagnetic, so MT cannot work. Visible dye with solvent wiping and a thin white nonaqueous developer is the portable white-light technique built for localized examinations.

checkQuestions.question: Why does standard practice prohibit running a visible-dye examination on a surface that will later be examined with fluorescent penetrant?
options: Residual visible dye can remain in discontinuities and quench or mask the fluorescent indication
Visible developers permanently stain the surface white
Visible penetrants chemically attack aluminum and stainless steel
Fluorescent penetrant cannot wet any surface previously cleaned with solvent
checkQuestions.explanation: Visible dye left in a flaw does not fluoresce — it can absorb energy and suppress the glow of fluorescent penetrant that enters the same flaw later, so a real discontinuity can go dark under UV-A. The selection consequence: choosing Type II today can foreclose a Type I re-examination tomorrow.

checkQuestions.question: A steel forging shows a clear linear MT indication, but a properly performed PT of the same location is clean. The most defensible interpretation is:
options: The MT indication must be nonrelevant, so the part can ship
The PT must have been performed incorrectly
PT is the more sensitive method, so the clean result governs
The discontinuity may be slightly subsurface or closed at the surface — conditions MT detects and PT cannot — so the MT indication is evaluated on its own merits
checkQuestions.explanation: PT responds only to flaws that are open and clean at the surface; MT responds to flux leakage from flaws at and slightly below the surface, including tightly closed or contaminant-filled cracks. A clean PT neither confirms nor refutes the MT finding — it narrows down what kind of flaw it can be, and the MT indication is dispositioned under MT acceptance criteria.

checkQuestions.question: A fracture-critical weld is examined by radiography and then by fluorescent PT. What is the engineering logic of specifying both?
options: Radiography verifies that the penetrant process was performed correctly
RT interrogates the weld volume but can miss tight surface cracks; PT covers the entire surface in every flaw orientation — the methods are complementary
PT finds the subsurface porosity that radiography cannot image
Using two methods halves the required sensitivity level of each
checkQuestions.explanation: Each method covers the other's blind zone. RT images volumetric conditions, but a tight, essentially volume-free surface crack can escape it; PT cannot see below the surface at all but sweeps the whole wetted surface at once. Pairing a volumetric method with a surface method is deliberate complementary coverage, not redundancy.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.0 (selection of the appropriate liquid penetrant testing method) and §1.3 (PT as method of choice)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (classification, material, and process requirements)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (types, methods, sensitivity levels, developer forms; qualified products)
ASTM E165/E165M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing for General Industry (general reference)
